steps.title: Sum of interior angles in a pentagon
steps.content:
The sum of interior angles in any pentagon is $(5-2) \times 180^{\circ} = 540^{\circ}$.

Given $\angle B = 139^{\circ}$ and $\angle D = 174^{\circ}$:
$$\angle A + 139^{\circ} + \angle C + 174^{\circ} + \angle E = 540^{\circ}$$$$\angle A + \angle C + \angle E = 227^{\circ}$$

steps.title: Parallel lines and exterior angle turns
steps.content:
Since $AB \parallel DE$, consider how direction changes as we traverse the pentagon from $B$ to $D$ through $C$. At each vertex, we turn by the exterior angle ($180^{\circ}$ minus the interior angle):
  • Exterior angle at $B$: $180^{\circ} - 139^{\circ} = 41^{\circ}$
  • Exterior angle at $C$: $180^{\circ} - \angle C$
  • Exterior angle at $D$: $180^{\circ} - 174^{\circ} = 6^{\circ}$
The total directional change from side $AB$ to side $DE$ is:
$$41^{\circ} + (180^{\circ} - \angle C) + 6^{\circ} = 227^{\circ} - \angle C$$
For parallel lines with opposite orientations (as required in this configuration), this total change must equal $180^{\circ}$.

steps.title: Solve for angle C
steps.content:
Setting the total directional change equal to $180^{\circ}$:
$$227^{\circ} - \angle C = 180^{\circ}$$$$\angle C = 47^{\circ}$$
